text: Musonius Rufus, the Roman Stoic teacher who influenced Epictetus, insisted that philosophy was not an intellectual exercise but a practice of living — and that anything interrupting rational self-governance was genuinely unnatural for a human being. He applied this with unusual consistency: indulgence in entertainment, luxury in food, avoidance of physical discomfort — all were, in his view, forms of allowing appetite to displace reason as the governing principle of life. Musonius was not an ascetic in the religious sense; he was a naturalist, arguing that the human animal's characteristic excellence was rational agency, and anything that routinely subordinated that agency to pleasure was a kind of species betrayal.
question: According to the passage, how did Musonius Rufus view indulgence in luxury and entertainment?

options.text: As morally sinful in a religious sense requiring penance
As allowing appetite to displace reason as the governing principle of life
As harmful to physical health and therefore contrary to nature
As acceptable when practiced in moderation alongside philosophy
